concepts.title: Equations in different forms of tangent
concepts.conceptBody:
Equations of Tangent in different forms 

1. Point Form 

The equation of the tangent to the ellipse $\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}+\frac{{{y}^{2}}}{{{b}^{2}}}(a>b)$ at a point $\left( {{x}_{1}}{{y}_{1}} \right)$ is

$ $

TIP : Replace ${{\text{x}}^{2}}$ by $\text{x}{{\text{x}}_{1}}$

${{y}^{2}}\,by\,y{{y}_{1}},$ in the equation of ellipse

NOTE : The equation of tangent at $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ can be obtained by replacing ${{\text{x}}^{2}}$ by $\text{x}{{\text{x}}_{1}}$,

${{\text{y}}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ by }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}{{\text{y}}_{1}}\text{, }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x }\!\!~\!\!\text{ by }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{x}+{{\text{x}}_{1}}}{2}\text{, }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\,\,\text{by }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{y}+{{\text{y}}_{1}}}{2}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and}\,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ xy }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\,\,\text{by }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\,\,\frac{\text{x}{{\text{y}}_{1}}+{{\text{x}}_{1}}\text{y}}{2}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

This is method is apply only when the equation of ellipse is a polynomial of second degree in $\text{x}$ and $\text{y}$.$ $

2. Slope Form 

(a) $y=mx+c$ ...(1)

meets the ellipse in points whose abscissae are given by

${{\text{x}}^{2}}\,\,\left( \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{b}}^{2}}\,\,+\,\,{{\text{a}}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}^{2}} \right)\,\,\,+\,\,2mc{{a}^{2}}\text{x}\,\,+\,\,{{\text{a}}^{2}}\left( {{\text{c}}^{2}}-{{\text{b}}^{2}} \right)=0$

In this case the straight line (1) is a tangent, and it becomes

$c\,\,=\,\,\pm \sqrt{{{\text{a}}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}^{2}}+{{\text{b}}^{2}}}$

In this case the straight line (1) is a tangent, and it becomes

$y=mx\pm \sqrt{{{a}^{2}}{{m}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}}$

This is the required equation.

Since, the radical sign on the right-hand of (2) may have either + or - prefixed to it, we see that there are two tangents to the ellipse having the same $\text{m}$, i.e., there are two tangents parallel to any given direction.

It will be found that the point of contact is the point

$\left( \frac{-{{a}^{2}}m}{\sqrt{{{a}^{2}}{{m}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}}},\frac{{{b}^{2}}}{\sqrt{{{a}^{2}}{{m}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}}} \right)$

(b) By a proof similar to that of the last article, it may be shewn that the straight line

$\text{x}\,\,\text{cos}\,\,\,\alpha \,\,+\,\,\text{y}\,\,\text{sin}\,\,\alpha \,\,=\,\,\text{p}$

touches the ellipse, if

${{\text{p}}^{2}}={{\text{a}}^{2}}\,\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\,\alpha \,\,+\,\,\,{{\text{b}}^{2}}\,\,\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\,\,\alpha $

(c) Similarly, it may be shewn that the straight line

$\text{lx}+\text{my}=\text{n}$

touches the ellipse, if ${{\text{a}}^{2}}{{\text{l}}^{2}}+{{\text{b}}^{2}}~{{\text{m}}^{2}}={{\text{n}}^{2}}.$

3. Parametric Form 

The coordinates of the point are $\left( \text{a}\,\,\text{cos}\,\text{ }\phi ,\text{b}\,\,\text{sin}\,\,\phi \right)$.

Substituting $\text{{x}'}=\text{a}\,\,\text{cos}\,\,\phi $ and $\text{{y}'}=\,\,\text{b}\,\,\text{sin}\,\,\phi $ in the equation, we have, as the required equation,

$\frac{x}{a}\,\,cos\,\,\phi \,\,+\,\,\frac{y}{b}\,\,sin\,\,\phi \,\,=\,\,1$ ...(1)

concepts.title: Intersection of tangents at paramteric points(phi and phi')
concepts.conceptBody:
The intersection of the tangents at the points $\phi $ and $\phi $

The intersection of the tangents at the points $\phi $ and ${\phi }'$.

The equations to be the tangents are

$\frac{\text{x}}{\text{a}}\cos \phi +\frac{\text{y}}{\text{b}}\sin \phi -1=0$

and $\frac{\text{x}}{\text{a}}\cos {{\phi }^{\prime }}+\frac{\text{y}}{\text{b}}\sin {{\phi }^{\prime }}-1=0$

The required point is found by solving these equations.

We obtain

$\frac{\frac{\text{x}}{\text{a}}}{\sin \phi -\sin {{\phi }^{\prime }}}=\frac{\frac{\text{y}}{\text{b}}}{\cos {{\phi }^{\prime }}-\cos \phi }=\frac{-1}{\sin {{\phi }^{\prime }}\cos \phi -\cos {{\phi }^{\prime }}\sin \phi }=\frac{1}{\sin \left( \phi -{{\phi }^{\prime }} \right)}$

i.e.,

$\frac{\text{x}}{2\text{a}\cos \frac{\phi +{{\phi }^{\prime }}}{2}\sin \frac{\phi -{{\phi }^{\prime }}}{2}}=\frac{\text{y}}{2~\text{b}\sin \frac{\phi +{{\phi }^{\prime }}}{2}\sin \frac{\phi -{{\phi }^{\prime }}}{2}}=\frac{1}{2\sin \frac{\phi -{{\phi }^{\prime }}}{2}\cos \frac{\phi -{{\phi }^{\prime }}}{2}}$

Hence, $\text{x}=\text{a}\frac{\cos \frac{1}{2}\left( \phi +{{\phi }^{\prime }} \right)}{\cos \frac{1}{2}\left( \phi -{{\phi }^{\prime }} \right)}\,\,and\,\text{y}=\text{b}\frac{\sin \frac{1}{2}\left( \phi +{{\phi }^{\prime }} \right)}{\cos \frac{1}{2}\left( \phi -{{\phi }^{\prime }} \right)}$

$\left\{\text{Tip to remember : } \,x=a\frac{\cos \frac{(\text{ sum })}{2}}{\cos \frac{\text{ (diff}\text{. })}{2}},y=b\frac{\sin \frac{(\text{ sum })}{2}}{\cos \left( \frac{\text{ diff }}{2} \right)} \right\}$

concepts.title: Length of Sub Tangent
concepts.conceptBody:
The length of the subtangent and subnormal 

 

Let the tangent and normal at $\text{P}$, the point $\left( \text{{x}'},\text{{y}'} \right)$, meet the axis in $\text{T}$ and $\text{G}$ respectively, and let $\text{PN}$ be the ordinate of $\text{P}$.

The equation to the tangent at $\text{P}$ is

$\frac{x{x}'}{{{a}^{2}}}+\frac{y{y}'}{{{b}^{2}}}=1$ ...(1)

To find where the straight line meets the axis, we put $\text{y}=0$ and have

$\text{x}=\frac{{{\text{a}}^{2}}}{{\text{{x}'}}}\text{, }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ CT}=\frac{{{\text{a}}^{2}}}{\text{CN}}$

i.e., $\text{CT}\cdot \text{CN}={{\text{a}}^{2}}=\text{C}{{\text{A}}^{2}}$ ...(2)

Hence, the subtangent NT $=\text{CT}-\text{CN}=\frac{{{\text{a}}^{2}}}{{\text{{x}'}}}-\text{{x}'}=\frac{{{\text{a}}^{2}}-{{\text{x}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }2}}}{{\text{{x}'}}}$

The equation to the normal is

$\frac{x-{x}'}{\frac{{{x}'}}{{{a}^{2}}}}=\frac{y-{y}'}{\frac{{{y}'}}{{{b}^{2}}}}$

To find where it meets the axis, we put $y=0$, and have

$\frac{x-{x}'}{\frac{{{x}'}}{{{a}^{2}}}}=\frac{-{y}'}{\frac{{{y}'}}{{{b}^{2}}}}=-{{b}^{2}}$

i.e., $CG=x={x}'-\frac{{{b}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}{x}'=\frac{{{a}^{2}}-{{b}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}{x}'={{e}^{2}}\cdot {x}'={{e}^{2}}\cdot CN$ ...(3)

Hence, the subnormal NG $=\text{CN}-\text{CG}=\left( 1-{{\text{e}}^{2}} \right)\text{CN}$ 
i.e., $\text{NG}:\text{NC}::1-{{\text{e}}^{2}}:1$ 
$::{{\text{b}}^{2}}:{{\text{a}}^{2}}$

Some properties of Ellipse 

  1. $\text{SG}=\text{e}.\text{SP}$, and the tangent and normal at $\text{P}$ bisect the external and internal angles between the focal distances of $P$.
  2. If $\text{SY}$ and $\text{{S}'{Y}'}$ be the perpendiculars from the foci upon the tangent at any point $\text{P}$ of the ellipse, then $\text{Y}$ and $\text{{Y}'}$ lie on the auxiliary circle, and $\text{SY}.\text{{S}'{Y}'}={{\text{b}}^{2}}$. Also CY and $\text{{S}'P}$ are parallel. 3. If the normal at any point $\text{P}$ meet the major and minor axes in $\text{G}$ and $\text{g}$, and if $\text{CF}$ be the perpendicular upon this normal, then $PF.PG={{b}^{2}}$ and $PF.Pg={{a}^{2}}$.
  3. The locus of the feet of the perpendiculars from the foci on any tangent to an ellipse is the auxiliary circle.
  4. The locus of the feet of the perpendiculars from the foci on any tangent to an ellipse is the auxiliary circle.
